title: That Jesus Christ is the Son of God and Truly Man, as Opposed to the Fancies of Heretics, Who Deny that He Took Upon Him True Flesh.

source.title: ANF05. Fathers of the Third Century: Hippolytus, Cyprian, Caius, Novatian, Appendix

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Argument.—That Jesus Christ is the Son of God and Truly Man, as Opposed to the Fancies of Heretics, Who Deny that He Took Upon Him True Flesh.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: But of this I remind you, that Christ was not to be expected in the Gospel in any other wise than as He was promised before by the Creator, in the Scriptures of the Old Testament; especially as the things that were predicted of Him were fulfilled, and those things that were fulfilled had been predicted.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: As with reason I might truly and constantly say to that fanciful—I know not what—of those heretics who reject the authority of the Old Testament, as to a Christ feigned and coloured up from old wives’ fables: “Who art thou?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Whence art thou?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: By whom art thou sent?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Wherefore hast thou now chosen to come?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Why such as thou art?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Or how hast thou been able to come?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Or wherefore hast thou not gone to thine own, except that thou hast proved that thou hast none of thine own, by coming to those of another?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: What hast thou to do with the Creator’s world?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: What hast thou to do with the Creator’s man?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: What hast thou to do with the image of a body from which thou takest away the hope of resurrection?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Why comest thou to another man’s servant, and desirest thou to solicit another man’s son?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Why dost thou strive to take me away from the Lord?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Why dost thou compel me to blaspheme, and to be impious to my Father?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Or what shall I gain from thee in the resurrection, if I do not receive myself when I lose my body?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If thou wishest to save, thou shouldest have made a man to whom to give salvation.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If thou desirest to snatch from sin, thou shouldest have granted to me previously that I should not fall into sin.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: But what approbation of law dost thou carry about with thee?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: What testimony of the prophetic word hast thou?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Or what substantial good can I promise myself from thee, when I see that thou hast come in a phantasm and not in a bodily substance?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: What, then, hast thou to do with the form of a body, if thou hatest a body?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Nay, thou wilt be refuted as to the hatred of bearing about the substance of a body, since thou hast been willing even to take up its form.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: For thou oughtest to have hated the imitation of a body, if thou hatedst the reality; because, if thou art something else, thou oughtest to have come as something else, lest thou shouldest be called the Son of the Creator if thou hadst even the likeness of flesh and body.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Assuredly, if thou hatedst being born because thou hatedst ‘the Creator’s marriage-union,’ thou oughtest to refuse even the likeness of a man who is born by the ‘marriage of the Creator.’”

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Neither, therefore, do we acknowledge that that is a Christ of the heretics who was—as it is said—in appearance and not in reality; for of those things which he did, he could have done nothing real, if he himself was a phantasm, and not reality.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Nor him who wore nothing of our body in himself, seeing “he received nothing from Mary;” neither did he come to us, since he appeared “as a vision, not in our substance.”

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Nor do we acknowledge that to be Christ who chose an ethereal or starry flesh, as some heretics have pretended.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Nor can we perceive any salvation of ours in him, if in him we do not even recognise the substance of our body; nor, in short, any other who may have worn any other kind of fabulous body of heretical device.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: For all such fables as these are confuted as well by the nativity as by the death itself of our Lord.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: For John says: “The Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us;” so that, reasonably, our body should be in Him, because indeed the Word took on Him our flesh.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: And for this reason blood flowed forth from His hands and feet, and from His very side, so that He might be proved to be a sharer in our body by dying according to the laws of our dissolution.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: And that He was raised again in the same bodily substance in which He died, is proved by the wounds of that very body, and thus He showed the laws of our resurrection in His flesh, in that He restored the same body in His resurrection which He had from us.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: For a law of resurrection is established, in that Christ is raised up in the substance of the body as an example for the rest; because, when it is written that “flesh and blood do not inherit the kingdom of God,” it is not the substance of the flesh that is condemned, which was built up by the divine hands that it should not perish, but only the guilt of the flesh is rightly rebuked, which by the voluntary daring of man rebelled against the claims of divine law.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Because in baptism and in the dissolution of death the flesh is raised up and returns to salvation, by being recalled to the condition of innocency when the mortality of guilt is put away.
